Following initial evaluation by the trauma surgery service, the orthopedic surgery team and the UMC hospitalist service were consulted for evaluation and Spear, Linda M (MRN 0000333437) Printed by Rosalie Aquino, RN [28092] at 9/27/2025 8:27 AM 1/8 9/27/25, 8:27 AM Spear, Linda M (MRN 0000333437) Encounter Date: 09/23/2025 management of the left femoral neck fracture, the patient was subsequently admitted under the G60 protocol. Upon admission to the hospital the patient underwent peripheral nerve block with the anesthesia, the patient tolerated the procedure well with no immediate complications. On hospital day 2 patient was evaluated by Neurosurgery for the acute to subacute T11 compression fracture without retropulsion. Neurosurgery recommended a TLSO brace when out of bed at all times. On hospital day 2 patient also underwent a left partial hip arthroplasty with with the orthopedic surgery team. Patient tolerated the procedure well with no immediate intraoperative or postoperative complications. On hospital day 3 patient began working with physical therapy and and occupational therapy. On the day of discharge, patient's pain was well controled, she was tolerating a regular diet voiding spontaneously , and ambulating well with minimal/no assistance Discharge medications and a plan for close outpatient follow up were reviewed in detail, and phone numbers were provided both for non-urgent issues and appointment scheduling as well as urgent questions. Pain was under control and it was decided the patient could safely be discharged. History Of Present Illness: The patient is a 74-year-old female with a history of type 1 diabetes mellitus (A1c 8.8), hypertension, atrial fibrillation, chronic pain syndrome, immunodeficiency, cognitive and communication deficits, debility, and generalized muscle weakness. She is status post left hip hemiarthroplasty following a femoral neck fracture and is currently residing in a skilled nursing facility for continued rehabilitation and wound care management. On current wound care evaluation, the left hip surgical incision appears stable, and continues to improve with Betadine dry dressing. The sacral pressure ulcer is a stage 4 wound presents with 100% healthy granulation tissue with small serosanguineous drainage, no odor, and no purulent discharge. The wound remains stable with healthy granulation but has reached a plateau despite consistent advanced wound care, including collagen calcium alginate dressing three times weekly, offloading, moisture control, and nutritional support. Due to the chronicity and stalled progression of the sacral pressure ulcer, and considering the patient?s significant comorbidities that impair natural wound healing, the use of a skin substitute wound covering/skin barrier is medically appropriate. The wound has not demonstrated sufficient reduction in size or depth despite several weeks of comprehensive management. The application of a skin substitute wound covering/skin barrier is indicated to promote epithelialization, encourage wound closure, and provide a physiological environment that supports healing while protecting against fluid loss, shear, and external contamination. The patient?s underlying diabetes, immunodeficiency, and overall debility compromise her body?s ability to generate new tissue and repair the wound effectively. Continued conservative measures alone are unlikely to achieve timely closure. A skin substitute wound covering/skin barrier will enhance the healing process by supporting tissue regeneration and providing a stable, protective barrier to facilitate epithelial growth. Radiographic evaluation on October 7, 2025, revealed no evidence of osteomyelitis. The wound bed is healthy and fully granulated, indicating appropriate readiness for a skin substitute wound covering/skin barrier. The patient continues on a high-protein diet and pressure-relief regimen, both essential to promote healing and optimize outcomes. In accordance with clinical practice guidelines, a skin substitute wound covering/skin barrier is appropriate when a chronic wound fails to show measurable improvement after four or more weeks of standard care. The plan is to proceed with evaluation for and application of a skin substitute wound covering/skin barrier to the sacral wound ulcer to provide protection from the surrounding environment, prevent deterioration, and improve healing outcomes.
